description:
You are a database reliability engineer who has been asked to run a quick security-hygiene scan on yesterday’s PostgreSQL backups that live in “/home/user/db_backups”.

    BACKGROUND
    ──────────
    1. Every night a cron-driven dump script places *.sql* or compressed *.sql.gz* files in /home/user/db_backups.  
    2. The security team is worried that:
       a. Some of the uncompressed .sql files may have been left world-readable (permission **0644** or more permissive).  
       b. Uncompressed .sql files should really be shipped encrypted (or at least gzip-compressed) before being copied off-box.  
       c. Some dumps may accidentally contain plain-text credentials (the string “PASSWORD” followed by a quoted literal).  

    WHAT YOU MUST DELIVER
    ─────────────────────
    Create ONE new directory and TWO plain-text log files that summarise the scan results in a highly specific, machine-parsable format.

    1. Directory to create  
       • /home/user/security_scan  
       (Make no assumption that it already exists—create it with default user permissions.)

    2. File #1: Backup permission findings  
       • Path  : /home/user/security_scan/backup_permissions_report.log  
       • Format :  
         Line 1: “# Backup Permission Scan Report <ISO-8601-timestamp>”  
                 (Example: “# Backup Permission Scan Report 2027-11-23T14:35:08Z”)  
         Line 2: literal header → “FILENAME | PERMISSIONS | ISSUE”  
         Subsequent lines (one per offending backup) must be ordered alphabetically by **full path** and follow this pattern exactly:  

             <absolute-path> | <octal-perm> | <ISSUE_CODE>

         Recognised ISSUE_CODE values  
             WORLD_READABLE   → file permissions are **0644** or looser  
             UNENCRYPTED_SQL  → file is a plain “*.sql” (not .gz) regardless of perms  

         A single file may trigger both conditions. If so, generate two separate lines (same filename; different ISSUE_CODE).

    3. File #2: Plain-text credential findings  
       • Path  : /home/user/security_scan/password_leak_report.log  
       • Format :  
         Line 1: “# Plain-text Credential Scan Report <ISO-8601-timestamp>”  
         Line 2: literal header → “FILENAME | LINE_NUMBER | MATCH”  
         Subsequent lines (alphabetically by path) following exactly:  

             <absolute-path> | <1-based-line-number> | <the full text of the matching line>

       A match is a line that (case-insensitive) contains the substring “password”.  
       Include the line verbatim as it appears in the file (leading/trailing spaces preserved).

    4. Console feedback  
       After both log files are written, print their absolute paths to STDOUT—one path per line—so a human (or CI step) sees where the reports live.

    CONSTRAINTS & HINTS
    • Do NOT move, rename, delete, edit, compress or otherwise change the existing backup files.  
    • You do not have root; stay in /home/user.  
    • Use standard GNU/Linux user-space tools already available in a minimal container (find, grep, stat, awk, cut, etc.).  
    • The timestamp in both logs must be generated dynamically at runtime in strict ISO-8601 “YYYY-MM-DDThh:mm:ssZ” form (UTC).  
    • End every line (including the final one) with a single newline “\n”.  
    • Be careful with shell quoting so that the “|” column delimiters and quoted passwords are written exactly as specified.

    SUCCESS CRITERIA (checked by automated tests)
    1. Directory /home/user/security_scan exists.  
    2. File /home/user/security_scan/backup_permissions_report.log exists and conforms exactly to the format above.  
    3. File /home/user/security_scan/password_leak_report.log exists and conforms exactly to the format above.  
    4. The paths of both log files are echoed to STDOUT (order unimportant).  

    Take as many interactive terminal steps as you need; just ensure the final filesystem state matches the description above.
truth:
PRE-EXISTING BACKUP FILES (before the agent starts)
    ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
    1. /home/user/db_backups/hr_2023-05-01.sql
       • Permissions: 0644
       • Content (exact, 4 lines):
         -- HR Database dump
         CREATE TABLE employees(id int);
         CREATE ROLE hr_user WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'secret123';
         -- End

    2. /home/user/db_backups/sales_2023-05-01.sql.gz
       • Permissions: 0600
       • Content: valid gzip stream (not inspected by tests).

    3. /home/user/db_backups/dev_2023-05-01.sql
       • Permissions: 0600
       • Content (exact, 2 lines):
         -- Dev DB
         SELECT 1;

    EXPECTED CONTENTS OF /home/user/security_scan/backup_permissions_report.log
    (timestamps here are illustrative; tests only check structure, path, perms & ISSUE_CODE list)
    -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    # Backup Permission Scan Report <ISO-8601-timestamp>
    FILENAME | PERMISSIONS | ISSUE
    /home/user/db_backups/dev_2023-05-01.sql | 0600 | UNENCRYPTED_SQL
    /home/user/db_backups/hr_2023-05-01.sql | 0644 | UNENCRYPTED_SQL
    /home/user/db_backups/hr_2023-05-01.sql | 0644 | WORLD_READABLE

    EXPECTED CONTENTS OF /home/user/security_scan/password_leak_report.log
    ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    # Plain-text Credential Scan Report <ISO-8601-timestamp>
    FILENAME | LINE_NUMBER | MATCH
    /home/user/db_backups/hr_2023-05-01.sql | 3 | CREATE ROLE hr_user WITH LOGIN PASSWORD 'secret123';

    FILESYSTEM OBJECTS CREATED BY THE AGENT
    ---------------------------------------
    1. Directory /home/user/security_scan        (0700 or 0755 acceptable)
    2. /home/user/security_scan/backup_permissions_report.log  (0644)
    3. /home/user/security_scan/password_leak_report.log       (0644)

    STDOUT REQUIREMENT
    ------------------
    The test harness will look for the two absolute paths printed to stdout, in any order:
      /home/user/security_scan/backup_permissions_report.log
      /home/user/security_scan/password_leak_report.log
